title: Why Do You Trust a Face in Under a Second?
published_at: TODO -- fill in before publish -- ISO datetime, set on actual publish
updated_at: TODO -- fill in before publish -- ISO datetime
summary: Princeton psychologists Janine Willis and Alexander Todorov studied how the human brain forms trustworthiness judgments from a face in 100 milliseconds. This is the psychology of first impressions and facial trust.
answer: TODO -- fill in before publish -- one paragraph direct answer; can adapt from script.md's cold open / closing reframe
duration_iso: TODO -- fill in before publish -- e.g. PT8M30S, fill in once the final render duration is known
key_findings: Todorov's lab didn't stop at a tenth of a second.
And this isn't just a lab curiosity that stays inside a lab.
There's a deeper question underneath all of this, and Todorov's own lab went looking for it too: if a tenth of a second is enough, what is the brain actually reading in that glimpse?
And the model held up under a real test.
article_markdown: TODO -- fill in before publish -- fresh long-form written article for reading (NOT the spoken script verbatim). Use script.md and sources.json as source material.

sources.title: Willis, J. & Todorov, A. (2006). First Impressions: Making Up Your Mind After a 100-ms Exposure to a Face. Psychological Science, 17(7), 592-598.
sources.publisher: 100ms face exposure judgments correlate highly with unlimited-time judgments; more time increases confidence, not accuracy of the verdict itself

sources.title: Todorov, A., Pakrashi, M. & Oosterhof, N.N. (2009). Evaluating Faces on Trustworthiness After Minimal Time Exposure. Social Cognition, 27(6), 813-833.
sources.publisher: Trustworthiness judgments begin forming at exposure times as low as 17-67ms

sources.title: Todorov, A., Mandisodza, A.N., Goren, A. & Hall, C.C. (2005). Inferences of Competence From Faces Predict Election Outcomes. Science, 308(5728), 1623-1626.
sources.publisher: Facial-competence judgments from a 1-second exposure predicted real congressional election outcomes at ~70% accuracy (68.8% Senate, 72.4% House)

sources.title: Todorov, A. (2017). Face Value: The Irresistible Influence of First Impressions. Princeton University Press.
sources.publisher: "Irresistible but faulty" framing -- judgments are automatic/hard to override but not reliable indicators of actual character; sourced from the researcher's own stated conclusion

sources.title: Oosterhof, N.N. & Todorov, A. (2008). The Functional Basis of Face Evaluation. Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences, 105(32), 11087-11092.
sources.publisher: Two-dimensional model of face evaluation (trustworthiness/valence + dominance); trustworthy-rated faces structurally resemble happy expressions and untrustworthy-rated faces resemble angry expressions on neutral faces ("emotion overgeneralization"); model cross-validated on newly generated faces rated by separate participants
